utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: David, welcome back. Come on in. Before we start our processing work today, I want to check in with you. How has the week been in terms of sleep, nightmares, flashbacks?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Sleep is still pretty broken. I'm getting maybe five hours on a good night, three or four on a bad one. The nightmares were three times this week — down from five last week though, so that's something. They're still the same theme — the convoy, the dust, Martinez's face. But one of them was different... it had an ending this time. Like, I actually got to him in the dream. That's never happened before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's interesting and potentially significant. After EMDR processing begins, people often report changes in how the trauma shows up in dreams — the narrative can shift, incorporate new elements, even resolve differently. The fact that you reached him in the dream may be connected to the processing work we started preparing for. We'll keep tracking that. What about daytime symptoms?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No full flashbacks this week. But I had one moment in the grocery store — Sunday afternoon. A cart dropped off one of those stack rows and hit the floor with a big metal crash. I went into that hypervigilant mode immediately. Heart racing, muscles tensed, scanning for exits, trying to figure out where the threat was. My wife was right next to me and she said my whole body changed. Like I went somewhere else.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How long did that state last?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe ten minutes to get fully back. The first minute or two were the worst — I was in it. Full sympathetic activation, like my body thought we were back there. Then I started to orient — "I'm in a grocery store, this is Oregon, it's 2026" — and the intensity started dropping. But the residual jumpiness lasted maybe another eight minutes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I notice you said "come back down" last time we talked about a similar experience, and today you described a more detailed process — orienting to the present, noting your location, the year. What did you use to regulate?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The breathing — four-seven-eight. Four count in, seven hold, eight out. And I grounded by naming five things I could see — the fluorescent lights, the frozen peas, my wife's red jacket, the floor tiles, the exit sign. Felt stupid doing it in the frozen food aisle, honestly. My wife knows what I'm doing at this point but other people probably thought I was having some kind of episode.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nothing stupid about using effective coping in a triggering situation. And let me reframe the "episode" concern — even if someone noticed, what they saw was a man pausing for a moment, breathing deliberately, looking around. That's what regulation looks like from the outside. From the inside it feels dramatic, but from the outside it's barely visible. How did your wife respond?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She's great. She put her hand on my back — she knows not to grab me suddenly — and just said quietly "you're here, you're safe." Didn't make a big deal. She's learned a lot from the family sessions.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Having a regulated co-regulator makes a real difference. Now — ten minutes to regulate from a startle response. At intake, David, you described those states lasting hours. Sometimes the rest of the day. And you described aggressive responses — throwing things, punching walls, leaving the house. Compare that to ten minutes, deliberate coping, staying present with your wife in the store.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Yeah. It's different. I'm not saying it's good yet but it's different.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It IS different. Measurably. How's the overall distress level compared to last session? If you average across the week, 0 to 10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe a six out of ten on average? There are moments that are lower. I played catch with my son on Saturday — just out in the backyard, throwing the football around — and I was genuinely present for like twenty minutes. I wasn't scanning, I wasn't bracing for anything, I was just... there with him. Watching him throw, coaching his spiral. He's eleven and he's getting tall and I was actually seeing him. That felt good. That felt like me before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "Like me before" — before deployment?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. The guy I was before I went over. My wife says she catches glimpses of him sometimes now. She didn't used to see him at all.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The ability to be present with your son — even for twenty minutes — that's what we're working toward. Those windows of presence will expand as we continue the processing work. The hypervigilance takes up cognitive bandwidth — when it decreases, you get that bandwidth back for connection, for enjoyment, for being a father and a husband.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's all I want, doc. I missed so much of his childhood because I was either deployed or a mess after I got back.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're here now. He's eleven. There's a lot of childhood left. Now — last session we did our preparation work. We identified the target memory as the convoy incident in Fallujah, September 2008. We installed your safe place resource — the cabin on the lake in eastern Oregon. We practiced the bilateral stimulation and you tolerated it well. Today, I'd like to begin processing that target memory if you're willing. How do you feel about that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Nervous. I've been thinking about it all week. Part of me wanted to cancel today. But I know this is the hard part and I know avoiding it is what's kept me stuck for twelve years. I've done the avoiding. It doesn't work. So... I'm ready. Let's do it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear the courage in that. And I want to remind you — you are in control of this process at all times. This is not like the trauma itself where things happened TO you and you had no control. Here, you are choosing to engage with the memory in a safe environment. If at any point the distress becomes too much, we stop. You can raise your hand or say "stop" and we pause immediately. We can slow down, resource, or close the processing entirely. This is at your pace. Sound good?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Good. I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: We'll use bilateral stimulation — the eye movements — to help your brain process the memory. You don't need to narrate out loud unless you choose to. Some clients process silently and just report what they notice during breaks. Others find it helpful to describe what's coming up. Either is fine. I'll check in periodically by asking "what comes up?" or "what do you notice?" Just give me whatever's there — an image, a thought, a sensation, an emotion. Don't censor or try to make it logical. Ready?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Let's do it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. I want you to bring up the target memory — the convoy incident. Bring up the worst moment, the image that represents the worst part of the experience. Take a moment to let it form. What do you see?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause, breathing changes] The truck in front of us. The Humvee. It was maybe forty meters ahead. I can see the back of it — the tailgate, the dust coming off the tires. And then... the flash. Under the vehicle. The whole front end lifts up. Debris everywhere. And then... Martinez. He's on the ground. Thrown clear somehow. I can see his face. He's looking at me — looking right at me — and I can't get to him. There's debris between us and I'm pinned by my door which buckled in the blast.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What emotions come up as you hold that image?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Guilt. Rage. Helplessness. The helplessness is the worst. Because I should have been in that truck. We switched positions that morning because I had the radio and the comms equipment was in our vehicle. If we hadn't switched, I'd have been up front and he'd have been where I was. Safe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And in your body, where do you feel all of that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Chest. Like a vise. Like something is sitting on my sternum. And my hands are tingling. Like they want to reach out but can't. My jaw is clenched — I can feel the muscles. And my legs are... restless. Like they want to run.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The negative cognition — the belief about yourself connected to this memory — what was it we identified in our preparation session?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "It should have been me." [pause] Or maybe... "I failed him." Yeah. "I failed him."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "I failed him." On a scale of 1 to 7, how true does that feel right now as you hold the image? Where 1 is completely false and 7 is completely true.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Seven. Completely true. In my bones.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And the positive cognition we'd like to move toward — "I did what I could in an impossible situation" — how true does that feel right now? Same 1 to 7 scale.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: One. Maybe a two. It doesn't feel true at all. It feels like something a therapist would say.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Fair enough. And the SUD — the subjective units of distress — holding that image and that belief "I failed him," how disturbing is it from 0 to 10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nine.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay David. I want you to hold that image — the truck, the flash, Martinez on the ground looking at you — hold the belief "I failed him" and notice where you feel it in your body — the chest, the hands, the jaw. Now follow my fingers. [bilateral stimulation begins]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Take a breath. Let it go. What comes up?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The sound. I forgot how loud it was. Or maybe I didn't forget — I blocked it. It was... concussive. Not like a movie explosion. More like the whole world getting punched. And then nothing — my ears were ringing so loud that everything else was just... silence. I couldn't hear anything. I was trying to get my bearings but there was dust everywhere. Like a sandstorm inside the vehicle.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Go with that. [BLS set 2 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath. What do you notice now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm remembering that I was disoriented. Completely. I didn't know which direction was up for a second. The vehicle had shifted — not rolled, but slid sideways. And I was yelling for my team — "Ramirez! Collins!" — and I couldn't hear my own voice. Just the ringing. Everything was white noise. I remember thinking "am I dead? Is this what it is?"

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Just notice that. The disorientation, the ringing, the question about whether you were alive. [BLS set 3 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's coming up now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [voice slightly unsteady] I'm... I'm in the vehicle still. My door is buckled. I'm trying to push it open and it won't move. I'm kicking it. And Collins is next to me — he's bleeding from his head but he's conscious. He's yelling something but I can't hear it. He's pointing. Pointing toward the front truck. Toward Martinez.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Go with that. Follow my fingers. [BLS set 4 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Breathe. What comes up?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [voice shaking] I got out. Not through my door — through Collins' side. I climbed over him. He pushed me. Said "go." And I ran. There was debris — metal, sand, something burning. I can smell it right now. The burning. And I got to him. I got to Martinez. I always — in the nightmares, in the flashbacks — I always remember not being able to reach him. Like there's this wall between us. But I did get to him. I got there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You got to him. Stay with that. [BLS set 5 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath. What's here?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying] He was on his side. His legs were... bad. I won't describe it. But he was conscious. He was looking at me. And I put pressure on — there was bleeding from his abdomen. I took off my jacket and pressed it there. And I was talking to him. I was saying "you're going to be okay, buddy, medics are coming, you're going to be okay." And he... he grabbed my arm. My left arm. He squeezed it. Hard. I forgot that part. For twelve years I forgot he squeezed my arm.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [softly] What does that mean to you — that he squeezed your arm?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying harder] He wasn't alone. He... he knew I was there. I was there with him. He wasn't alone on the ground in that place. He knew someone was with him. [long pause, breathing] I didn't leave him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You didn't leave him. Go with that. [BLS set 6 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath. What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes with both hands] I'm remembering more. The medics arrived — I don't know how long it was. Felt like an hour, probably three minutes. And Sergeant Collins pulled me away. I wouldn't let go. I was still pressing on Martinez's abdomen and Collins was pulling my shoulders saying "let them work, Park, let them work." And I was — I was fighting Collins. I didn't want to leave. They had to physically pull me off.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What does that tell you about the belief "I failed him"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long silence] I... didn't fail him. They had to drag me away from him. I didn't abandon him — I didn't run — I was there until someone physically removed me. I was fighting to stay. [pause] Why don't I ever remember that part? I only ever remember the moment of the blast and his face on the ground. That's the whole story my brain tells me. Blast, face, helplessness, failure. But there was so much more between those moments.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Traumatic memories often get frozen at the worst moment — the point of maximum helplessness and horror. The full sequence — what happened before and after that frozen frame — gets disconnected. Your brain stored the blast and the helplessness as THE memory. But what you're doing right now — right now in this processing — is allowing your brain to integrate the full experience. Not just the worst frame, but the whole story. Including your actions, your courage, your loyalty.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I stayed with him. The whole time. Until the medics took over. [pause] He survived, you know. For six hours. He died at the field hospital. But those six hours — I was the last person he saw who cared about him. Before they put him under for surgery. He saw my face.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's go with that. [BLS set 7 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What comes up now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I feel... lighter in my chest. The vise is looser. Not gone but looser. And I'm thinking about what his wife — Maria — told me at the memorial. She found me after the service. She took my hand and she said "Danny talked about you all the time. He said you always had his back. He said you were the best thing about his unit." And I couldn't — at the time, I couldn't hear that. I was drinking so heavily at that point that I barely remember the service. But her words stuck somewhere. And right now they feel... they feel different. They feel possible. Like maybe she was telling me the truth and not just being nice to a grieving soldier.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How does the belief "I failed him" feel right now? On that 1-7 scale?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [thinking] Maybe a four. I know I didn't fail him in the way I always thought. I was there. I did what I could. But... he still died. And I still feel like if the positions hadn't switched... if I'd been in that truck...

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's go with that — the "if only." [BLS set 8 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's here now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] If I'd been in that truck, I'd probably be dead. And Martinez would have been the one running toward me. He would have done the same thing. He would have gotten to me. He was that kind of soldier. [pause] We didn't choose who was in which truck. It was logistics. The radio equipment. It wasn't cowardice. It wasn't luck. It was just... the arrangement of a Tuesday morning.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The arrangement of a Tuesday morning. How does your body feel right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My chest is... open. Breathing is easier. Hands have stopped tingling. I'm tired. Really tired. Like I ran ten miles. But the vise — it's not gripping.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Let's do one more set and then we'll begin closing. I want you to bring up the original image — the moment after the blast — and tell me what comes up now when you see it. [BLS set 9 — approximately 25 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's still painful. The image is still there. But it's more like... a sad memory instead of a threat. Before, when I brought up that memory, my whole body went into combat mode. Right now I'm sad but I'm not afraid. I can see the whole thing now, not just the worst frame. I can see the blast AND myself getting out of the vehicle AND running to him AND holding him AND the medics arriving. It's a complete story instead of a frozen moment of horror.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: SUD level now, 0 to 10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe a five. Still hurts. But it hurts like grief now, not like an emergency. There's a difference.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's a huge difference. Grief is processable. Emergency is not — emergency keeps you stuck in survival mode. We moved from a nine to a five in one processing session. That's significant movement. The remaining distress — the grief — is appropriate. We're not trying to make this memory feel good. Martinez died. That's a real loss. But we're moving you from traumatic re-experiencing — where the memory ambushes you and hijacks your nervous system — to integrated grief, where you can remember him, feel sad, and stay in the present.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [exhale] Yeah. That's... that's what I want. I want to be able to think about him without my body going to war.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: We'll continue processing next session. The goal is to bring that SUD down further and strengthen the positive cognition — "I did what I could in an impossible situation." Let me ask — how does that belief feel now, 1-7?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe a four. Up from a one. I can feel the truth of it now. I can see the evidence of it in the memory. I got out of that vehicle and I ran to him and I stayed. That IS doing what I could.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That IS the evidence. Your own actions, which you couldn't access before because the memory was frozen. Now — I want to close the processing and install your safe place to make sure you leave here regulated. Can you bring up your safe place image — the cabin, the lake?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [closing eyes] Yeah. I'm there. Sitting on the dock. Early morning. Mist on the water. It's quiet — just birds and the occasional fish breaking the surface. The wood is warm under my legs from the morning sun. My son is still sleeping in the cabin behind me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Hold that. Notice how your body feels in that place. Notice the safety, the quiet, the warmth. [BLS — short set, approximately 15 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Strengthen it. Make it brighter, more vivid. [BLS — short set, approximately 15 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Take a breath. How are you feeling right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Tired. Really tired. Like I've been through something, which I guess I have. But okay. More okay than I expected to feel after doing that. I was bracing for... I don't know. A total breakdown. Falling apart. But I feel like something shifted instead.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Something did shift. What came up today was very important, David. Your memory filled in the parts that got disconnected by the trauma — that you got out of the vehicle, that you ran to your friend, that you provided medical aid, that you stayed with him, that you were physically pulled away by other personnel because you wouldn't leave his side. The story your brain has been telling you for twelve years — "I failed him," "I couldn't reach him," "I was helpless" — was an incomplete memory. It was frozen at the worst moment. The full truth is more complex, and it includes your courage, your loyalty, and your love for your brother.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet, wiping eyes again] Brother. Yeah. He was my brother.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And he knew that. His wife told you that. He squeezed your arm. He knew you were there. That matters.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [nods, can't speak for a moment]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take your time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [deep breath] I need to sit with this for a while. It's a lot. Twelve years of one story and then today it changes. My brain needs time to catch up.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Absolutely. And I want to prepare you for what might happen between now and next session. You may notice the memory surfacing differently — it might come up more often in the next few days, but it should feel less acute, more like the memory is "updating" rather than attacking you. You might have dreams that feel processing-related rather than nightmares — the content might shift, new elements might appear, the narrative might change. That's normal and it's a sign the processing is continuing between sessions.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like the dream this week where I reached him. That might have been... preview processing?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Possibly. Your brain may have already been preparing for today's work. You might also experience some emotional fatigue for the next day or two — processing this kind of material takes enormous energy. Be gentle with yourself. Rest if you need to. Let your wife know that you did processing work today so she can support you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She knows what today was. She was nervous this morning too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. If distress escalates — if you have a flashback or a significant urge moment — use your safe place first. Then the breathing. If it's still elevated, reach out to me. I'm available between sessions for brief check-ins by phone. You have that number?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I do. I haven't used it before but it's good to know it's there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Use it if you need it. No judgment. This is big work. Now — one more thing before we wrap up. I want to acknowledge something. You said at the beginning that part of you wanted to cancel today. That you've been thinking about this all week. And you came anyway. You sat in this chair and you faced the worst moment of your life deliberately and with courage. The same courage that got you out of that vehicle and running toward your friend twelve years ago — that's the same courage that brought you here today. You haven't lost it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Thank you, doc. [voice thick] That was... hard. Really hard. But good. Different kind of hard than usual. Not the "hard" of a nightmare ambushing me at 2 AM. The "hard" of choosing to go through something. There's more... dignity in it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There is. And you're going to be okay. Drink water today, rest, be with your family. We'll continue next Monday. Same time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same time. [standing up] Hey doc — one thing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yeah?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I want my son to know about Martinez someday. Not the death — but who he was. I want to be able to tell those stories without losing it. Is that something we're working toward?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Absolutely. Being able to share the memory of someone you loved — with appropriate emotion, not overwhelming trauma — that's integrated grief. That's where we're headed. You'll be able to tell your son about your brother.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [nods] Good. That's what I want. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take care, David. See you Monday.
